evidence_quotes.quote: misleading marketing making me believe that I've purchased a videogame, but instead I was purchasing a temporary license to a videogame, namely The Crew.
Ubisoft has ended support for the game, which invalidated my perceived ownership of my copy.
I would like publishers to transparently mark their products with "Live Service" tag or other kind of warning that would inform me about the product being a temporary access.
Instead of just shutting down the serves for the videogame forever, they allow people who purchased their game to host their own sessions.

paragraphs.text: Good day, I am a civil engineer from Czech Republic. I am concerned about misleading marketing making me believe that I've purchased a videogame, but instead I was purchasing a temporary license to a videogame, namely The Crew. Ubisoft has ended support for the game, which invalidated my perceived ownership of my copy. I would like publishers to transparently mark their products with "Live Service" tag or other kind of warning that would inform me about the product being a temporary access. For videogames that are not supposed to be temporary, I would suggest looking at The Spellbreaker game. Instead of just shutting down the serves for the videogame forever, they allow people who purchased their game to host their own sessions. I believe that we should expect the same standard for videogames that we expect from other digital and electronic products.
